subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Table of Twice-Differentiable f on [2,13] — Estimate f'(4), FTC Integral, Left Riemann Sum & Tangent/Secant Inequalities
stimulus:
Let $f$ be a function that is twice differentiable for all real numbers. The table below gives values of $f$ for selected points in the closed interval $2 \le x \le 13$.

| $x$ | 2 | 3 | 5 | 8 | 13 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f(x)$ | 1 | 4 | $-2$ | 3 | 6 |

parts.prompt: Estimate $f'(4)$. Show the work that leads to your answer.

sample_responses.commentary: Symmetric difference quotient centered at $x = 4$ using the table values at $x = 3$ and $x = 5$.
Uses a non-symmetric interval; the table provides values at $x = 3$ and $x = 5$ which bracket $x = 4$ symmetrically.

parts.prompt: Evaluate $\int_2^{13}(3 - 5f'(x))\, dx$. Show the work that leads to your answer.

scoring_points.description: Uses the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: applies FTC to $\int_2^{13} f'(x)\,dx = f(13) - f(2)$.

sample_responses.commentary: Linearity of the integral separates the constant and applies FTC to the $f'$ term.
Treats the integrand as constant; must use FTC for $\int f'(x)\,dx$, not evaluate at a sample point.

required_elements: Uses the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

sample_responses.commentary: Sign error: FTC gives $f(13) - f(2) = 6 - 1 = 5$, not $6 + 1 = 7$.

parts.prompt: Use a left Riemann sum with subintervals indicated by the data in the table to approximate $\int_2^{13} f(x)\, dx$. Show the work that leads to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Left Riemann sum $= f(2)(1) + f(3)(2) + f(5)(3) + f(8)(5) = 1 + 8 - 6 + 15 = 18$.

scoring_points.description: Sets up correct left Riemann sum
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct left Riemann sum structure with four subintervals $[2,3]$, $[3,5]$, $[5,8]$, $[8,13]$ using left endpoints.

sample_responses.commentary: Uses left endpoint function values with correct subinterval widths $1, 2, 3, 5$.
Uses right endpoints; question specifies a left Riemann sum.

required_elements: Sets up correct left Riemann sum

sample_responses.commentary: Simply sums the function values without multiplying by subinterval widths; each term must be $f(x_i) \cdot \Delta x_i$.

parts.prompt: Suppose $f'(5) = 3$ and $f''(x) < 0$ for all $x$ in the closed interval $5 \le x \le 8$. Use the line tangent to the graph of $f$ at $x = 5$ to show that $f(7) \le 4$. Use the secant line for the graph of $f$ on $5 \le x \le 8$ to show that $f(7) \ge \frac{4}{3}$.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Tangent line $y = -2 + 3(x-5)$; $f(7) \le -2 + 3(2) = 4$. Secant line slope $= 5/3$; $f(7) \ge -2 + \frac{5}{3}(2) = \frac{4}{3}$. Both inequalities follow from concavity.

sample_responses.commentary: Point-slope form using $f(5) = -2$ from the table and $f'(5) = 3$ as given.
Uses the estimated $f'(4) = -3$ instead of the given $f'(5) = 3$.

scoring_points.description: Shows $f(7) \le 4$: since $f''(x) < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph is concave down, so tangent line lies above the curve; $f(7) \le -2 + 3(2) = 4$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: since $f''(x) < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph is concave down, so the tangent line at $x = 5$ lies above the graph; evaluating at $x = 7$: $-2 + 3(2) = 4$, hence $f(7) \le 4$.

sample_responses.response: Since $f''(x) < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph of $f$ is concave down, so the tangent line lies above the curve. At $x = 7$: tangent line value $= -2 + 3(7-5) = 4$. Therefore $f(7) \le 4$.
sample_responses.commentary: Concave down means tangent line is an overestimate; evaluates at $x = 7$.

sample_responses.response: Since $f''(x) < 0$, the tangent line lies below the curve, so $f(7) \ge 4$.
sample_responses.commentary: Reverses the concavity conclusion; for concave-down functions, tangent lines are above the graph, giving an upper bound.

required_elements: Shows $f(7) \le 4$: since $f''(x) < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph is concave down, so tangent line lies above the curve; $f(7) \le -2 + 3(2) = 4$

scoring_points.description: Writes secant line through $(5, f(5))$ and $(8, f(8))$: slope $= \frac{3-(-2)}{8-5} = \frac{5}{3}$; $y = -2 + \frac{5}{3}(x-5)$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: secant line through $(5, -2)$ and $(8, 3)$ with slope $\dfrac{5}{3}$: $y = -2 + \dfrac{5}{3}(x-5)$.

sample_responses.commentary: Uses table values $f(5) = -2$ and $f(8) = 3$ to find the secant slope.
Acceptable to write the secant line and directly evaluate at $x = 7$.

scoring_points.description: Shows $f(7) \ge \frac{4}{3}$: since $f''(x) < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph is concave down, so secant line lies below the curve; $f(7) \ge -2 + \frac{5}{3}(2) = -2 + \frac{10}{3} = \frac{4}{3}$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: since $f''(x) < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph is concave down, so the secant line lies below the graph on $(5,8)$; at $x = 7$: secant value $= -2 + \frac{5}{3}(2) = \frac{4}{3}$, hence $f(7) \ge \frac{4}{3}$.

sample_responses.response: Since $f'' < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph is concave down, so the secant line lies below the curve. At $x = 7$: $-2 + \dfrac{5}{3}(2) = -2 + \dfrac{10}{3} = \dfrac{4}{3}$. Therefore $f(7) \ge \dfrac{4}{3}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the concavity result: concave down $\Rightarrow$ secant below graph $\Rightarrow$ lower bound.
Gives the bound without justifying why the secant is a lower bound (must cite concave down / $f'' < 0$).

required_elements: Shows $f(7) \ge \frac{4}{3}$: since $f''(x) < 0$ on $[5,8]$, the graph is concave down, so secant line lies below the curve; $f(7) \ge -2 + \frac{5}{3}(2) = -2 + \frac{10}{3} = \frac{4}{3}$
